old_claim.note: Sailing provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Triremes carried a square-rigged mainsail and boat-sail for passage and could operate under sail and oar, while their defining combat propulsion remained the oar crew.

source_shape.source_locator: Single-page poster, sea-trials section: Olympias was equipped like ancient ships with a square-rigged mainsail and boat-sail and was tested under both oar and sail.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Trireme Trust summary documents an ancient-style two-sail rig and practical operation under wind power as well as oars.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Sails expanded passage capability but the source separately identifies 170 oars as the defining propulsion system, supporting an enabling rather than required classification.

ontology_before: A generic sailing association did not distinguish wind-assisted passage from the vessel's defining manual combat propulsion.
ontology_after: Sailing remains a bounded enabler for the trireme's passage rig while manual boat propulsion is modeled separately as required.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved and strengthened by graph invariant 2026-07-16-greek-trireme-boundary: sailing remains enabling.
would_reject_if: Evidence shows Greek triremes lacked sails entirely or, conversely, that wind power rather than the three-level oar system defined their combat maneuvering.
short_reason: The reconstruction evidence directly supports sails for passage while preserving the oar system as the defining propulsion architecture.
